text: L-Asparagine monohydrate (C4H8N2O3·H2O), chemical formula C4H10N2O4, molecular weight 150.14, is a pure molecular crystal hydrate (amino acid with incorporated water molecule) and experimentally demonstrated as a piezoelectric biomolecular crystal. It crystallizes in the non-centrosymmetric, chiral orthorhombic space group P212121 (No. 19), with unit cell a = 5.58370 Å, b = 9.76460 Å, c = 11.74040 Å, Z = 4. The crystal was grown using a molded, substrate-free fabrication technique at room temperature from saturated aqueous solutions. L-asparagine monohydrate shows piezoelectric shear response due to its non-centrosymmetric space group symmetry. The crystal structure (CCDC 2325682) was determined by single-crystal X-ray diffraction at 179.99 K with an R factor of 2.88%, a density of 1.558 g/cm3, and a block habit. The piezoelectric and electromechanical properties were experimentally measured and reported by Hari et al. in Physical Review Letters (2024) as part of a comprehensive study on molded, solid-state biomolecular assemblies with programmable electromechanical properties.
experimental_method: Piezoelectric charge coefficient (d33) measurement, open circuit voltage measurement, stress-strain compression testing
property_ref_title: Molded, Solid-State Biomolecular Assemblies with Programmable Electromechanical Properties

absorption.correction_details: CrysAlisPro empirical absorption correction using spherical harmonics

mechanical.handling_difficulty: Very fragile - difficult to carry out contact characterization
mechanical.notes: Assemblies prone to breakage due to sharp distinct edges between single crystals

piezoelectric.piezoresponse_type: Shear permitted by space group P212121
piezoelectric.measurement_notes: Modified molding techniques needed for fragile materials

microscale_structure.packing_type: Assemblies of single crystals with sharp distinct edges
microscale_structure.cohesiveness: Less cohesive than some other amino acids
microscale_structure.morphology_notes: Fragile assembly prone to fracture at grain boundaries

sustainability_advantages.biodegradability: Decomposes into nontoxic and biocompatible wastes
sustainability_advantages.toxic_components: None - no lead, barium, bismuth, or niobium
sustainability_advantages.comparison_to_ceramics: Outperforms conventional piezoelectrics in eco-friendliness, cost-effectiveness, biodegradability

key_findings.first_report: First molded, substrate-free amino acid-based piezoelectric material
key_findings.methodology_novelty: Novel molding technique for fabricating amino acid-based piezoelectric materials as rigid molds
key_findings.scalability: Facile, scalable, substrate-free manner allowing any desired shape and size
key_findings.customization: Electromechanical properties embedded by fine-tuning chemistry and space group

synthesis_and_characterization.crystallization_time: Variable (typically days to weeks for slow evaporation)

hydrogen_bonding_network.water_role: Integrated water molecule participates in hydrogen bonding network
hydrogen_bonding_network.network_structure: 3D hydrogen bonding network stabilizing crystal structure

structural_uniqueness.water_integration: Water molecule incorporated in stoichiometric ratio (monohydrate)
structural_uniqueness.biomolecular_nature: Amino acid derivative with biological relevance
structural_uniqueness.chiral_structure: Chiral orthorhombic system with biological L-isomer

future_development.current_status: Fragile - requires modified molding techniques for enhanced macroscopic mechanical strength
future_development.research_directions: Work with fragile amino acids to enhance their mechanical properties
future_development.broader_application: Technique extensible to other eco-friendly molecular crystals
future_development.target_goal: Phase in biomolecular piezoelectrics as high-performance, eco-friendly alternatives to ceramics

publication_details.title: Molded, Solid-State Biomolecular Assemblies with Programmable Electromechanical Properties
publication_details.institution: Department of Chemical Sciences, Bernal Institute, University of Limerick, Ireland
funding: European Union ERC Starting Grant No. 101039636
